role_summary: SNCC organizer and photographer in Alabama and Mississippi from 1963; staff photographer for Black Star photo agency; co-founder (1981) of Ganados del Valle, the New Mexico rural Hispanic cooperative; MacArthur Foundation fellow, 1990.
ai_reading: Varela's top semantic neighbors by aggregated chunk count are Dr. Doris Derby (entry 32, count 29), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, count 25), Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53, count 20), Mateo Camarillo (entry 86, count 17), and H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, count 17). The Derby and Garman Robinson pairings track the SNCC-photographer-and-organizer reading the cultural record already carries. The surprise is lower in the same list: Mateo Camarillo (entry 86) and Gloria Arellanes (entry 47, count 14) are Mexican-American and Chicano-movement voices, not SNCC, and the embedding pulls them up near the SNCC cohort, so the vector space does surface her Chicana and land-grant arc rather than flattening it into a Deep South SNCC signature. Her sharpest axis reading runs the other way from the public framing: on the Nonviolence as Theology versus Armed Self-Defense axis she sits at +0.43, toward the Armed Self-Defense pole, for a figure cataloged as a SNCC literacy-and-photography worker. Her own testimony corroborates the placement; she says she and others who came in by late 1962 and early 1963 were not philosophically committed to nonviolence, that it was a strategy, and that she brought a gun South to defend herself. On the Individual Conscience versus Collective Discipline axis she reads at -0.42, toward Individual Conscience, which sits in tension with the four-decade cooperative-building work that followed.
biographical_paragraph: Varela was born on January 1, 1940, in Newell, Pennsylvania [src: 1]. She reached the southern movement through the Catholic student left, working on the national staff of the Young Christian Students and meeting SNCC organizers at National Student Association congresses, then joined SNCC around 1963 after Casey Hayden recruited her to the Atlanta office [src: 6]. Her first field assignment was adult-literacy work in Selma, Alabama, building reading materials for residents facing the Alabama voter-registration test, before she moved to Mississippi and learned documentary photography to produce the cooperative-organizing booklets and filmstrips she distributed across SNCC's Delta projects [src: 6]. Julian Bond's 2011 University Press of Mississippi book This Light of Ours: Activist Photographers of the Civil Rights Movement places her among the photographers of the movement [src: 2]. After SNCC, she moved to New Mexico and organized in the Hispanic land-grant communities of the upper Chama valley; in 1981 she helped found Ganados del Valle, a wool-and-sheep cooperative in Los Ojos, and in 1990 she received a MacArthur Foundation fellowship [src: 3]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 81 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Maria Varela
interview_snippets.lead_in: Varela traces her first contact with the movement to a National Student Association congress she attended as a Young Christian Students staffer, where SNCC sit-in participants pressed the body to take a stand:
interview_snippets.quote: So in that process is where I heard SNCC folks, student on violent coordinating committee sit in participants and leaders who came because they wanted the NSA to go on record.

interview_snippets.speaker: Maria Varela
interview_snippets.lead_in: On her first SNCC assignment, adult-literacy work in Selma supporting a priest who wanted residents able to pass the registration test:
interview_snippets.quote: father really wanted to have some literacy training. He felt, because then people could take that 21 question voter registration test. It's a ridiculous test.

interview_snippets.speaker: Maria Varela
interview_snippets.lead_in: On where the SNCC staff who arrived in late 1962 and 1963 stood on nonviolence:
interview_snippets.quote: Some of us who came in by late 62 early 63, we weren't philosophically committed to nonviolence. It was a strategy. You know, we, I know I brought my gun down because I was going to defend myself.

interview_snippets.speaker: Maria Varela
interview_snippets.lead_in: On what set SNCC's photography department apart from the image-making of the other civil rights organizations:
interview_snippets.quote: our purpose as SNCC photographers was to show not the celebrities in the movement, but to show the local folks who were the leaders, without which the movement could not move.

interview_snippets.speaker: Maria Varela
interview_snippets.lead_in: On what Black Power meant to her and how it carried into her New Mexico cooperative work:
interview_snippets.quote: People empowering themselves to create these institutions. And if they wouldn't let you in, you create your own.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner
interview_snippets.lead_in: Fellow SNCC staffer Dorothy Zellner, recalling how central cars were to field work, points to a Varela story from the Hands on the Freedom Plow anthology Zellner co-edited (the transcript renders Klan as clan):
interview_snippets.quote: And María Varela has a story in there, in Hansen, where she's driving, trying to escape the clan at 110 miles an hour, cars and loom very heavily under the story.

movement_context: Varela's route into the movement ran through the Catholic student left rather than the campus radicalism the period is usually narrated through. On the national staff of the Young Christian Students she worked the National Student Association congresses, where she first heard SNCC sit-in participants argue the southern student schools onto the record in support of the sit-ins; in her telling she was the office worker through whom resolutions moved [src: 1]. Tom Hayden invited the Young Christian Students to send a representative to the June 1962 Port Huron gathering as part of an effort to broaden the founding meeting beyond the existing student left; Varela attended, typed the meeting's minutes, and recalls being asked to help resolve points of language in the document, though she is tentative in her own account about how much she shaped the text [src: 1, 5]. She joined SNCC around 1963 after Casey Hayden wrote asking for help in the Atlanta office, and her first field assignment was not photography but adult literacy in Selma, Alabama, supporting a French-Canadian priest at an African-American Catholic mission and teaching residents to read against the Alabama voter-registration test [src: 1]. The photography came later and instrumentally: needing images of Black people running their own cooperatives for the literacy booklets and filmstrips she was producing, she spent a week in 1965 learning to shoot, develop, and print from the photographer Matt Herron in New Orleans, and she did not at the time identify herself as a photographer, describing the camera as in the service of whatever SNCC organizers in the communities needed [src: 1]. SNCC ran the only photography department of the major civil rights organizations, with roughly nine field photographers; Varela and Dr. Doris Derby (catalog entry 32) were among the women in that group, and Varela frames the department's purpose as documenting the local people who led the movement rather than its visible celebrities [src: 1, 6]. Her field photography ran into the second half of the 1960s, including the 1966 James Meredith March Against Fear and, after she had begun moving toward the Southwest, the 1968 Poor People's Campaign, where she photographed the Latino delegations [src: 1]. Through the 1966-1968 reorganization that put SNCC under an all-Black executive, Varela was not expelled; she describes a personalized letter from the leadership telling her to keep working with Fannie Lou Hamer's campaign, though her foundation funding dried up as she declined to repudiate Black Power [src: 1]. She moved to New Mexico to work for Reies Lopez Tijerina, the land-grant leader whose Alianza Federal de Mercedes had carried out the June 1967 Rio Arriba County courthouse raid at Tierra Amarilla, and then shifted from Tijerina's Albuquerque operation to community organizing in the upper Chama valley, beginning with a rural health clinic that first saw patients in 1969 [src: 1, 8]. From that base she helped build a cooperative economy around the Navajo-Churro heritage sheep and traditional Rio Grande weaving; the enterprises consolidated as Ganados del Valle, founded in 1981 in Los Ojos with land-grant community members, and the cooperative's grazing-rights advocacy brought it into conflict with the U.S. Forest Service and environmental organizations across the following years [src: 8]. The 1990 MacArthur Fellowship cited that rural cooperative-building work [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: Varela's New Mexico organizing extended past the founding of Ganados del Valle into a broader rural community-development practice; she has worked from her Los Ojos base in the Chama valley across the decades following the 1981 cooperative, and in the oral history she frames the whole arc as roughly thirty years of building alternative institutions around health care, land, and livestock [src: 1, 8]. Her contributing essay to the 2010 University of Illinois Press volume Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC, edited by Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (catalog entry 83), Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson (catalog entry 7), Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner (catalog entry 30), places her account among those of the women SNCC field staff in the standard primary-source compilation of that record [src: 10]. Dorothy Zellner, one of that volume's editors, recounts in her own oral history a Varela story from the book, a high-speed drive to outrun the Klan, which corroborates Varela's place in the SNCC field network [src: 11]. The David Cline oral history at catalog entry 81, recorded in 2016 in Pasadena, California for the Civil Rights History Project (AFC 2010/039), and her inclusion in the Julian Bond volume This Light of Ours anchor the institutional record of her movement photography [src: 1, 2].

gallery.alt: Participants carrying American flags during the 1965 civil rights march from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama
gallery.caption: Participants, some carrying American flags, marching from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama, in the federally protected March 21-25, 1965 voting rights march that followed the March 7 Bloody Sunday attack on the Edmund Pettus Bridge. Varela's first SNCC assignment was adult-literacy work in Selma in 1963 and 1964; she learned documentary photography in 1965 and her field camera work is documented later, including the 1966 James Meredith March Against Fear and the 1968 Poor People's Campaign. This image marks the Selma campaign she had worked in before the march. Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Digital ID cph.3c33090).
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Library of Congress)
gallery.license: Public domain (published in the United States between 1931 and 1977 without a copyright notice)

gallery.alt: Alabama state troopers attacking civil rights demonstrators on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, March 7, 1965
gallery.caption: Federal Bureau of Investigation photograph of Alabama state troopers attacking demonstrators on the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Alabama, on March 7, 1965, the event known as Bloody Sunday. The attack drew national television coverage and forced the federal intervention that produced the protected March 21-25 Selma-to-Montgomery march. Selma was where Varela had been assigned to adult-literacy organizing in 1963 and 1964, the town she describes in her oral history as the most repressive place SNCC sent her.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from National Park Service Civil Rights travel website)
gallery.license: Public domain (work product of a federal government agency)

gallery.alt: A horned Navajo-Churro ram on rangeland in the American Southwest
gallery.caption: A Navajo-Churro ram, the heritage sheep breed central to the rural Hispanic and Native sheep-ranching economy of northern New Mexico that the Ganados del Valle cooperative was founded in 1981 to sustain. The cooperative's traditional Rio Grande-style weaving work, organized through the Tierra Wools enterprise in Los Ojos, New Mexico, runs on the Navajo-Churro fleece, and the cooperative's grazing-rights advocacy across the 1980s and 1990s brought it into long-running conflict with the U.S. Forest Service and the Sierra Club over public-lands grazing access.
